name: Shaw, Tyler H.
recent_1: Sharp, W. H., Jackson, K. M., & Shaw, T. H. (2026). Guilty until proven innocent: Experiencing errors in training promotes relationship equity and trust calibration. Applied Ergonomics, 136, 104770. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104770
recent_1_synopsis: This study examined how experiencing automation errors during training affects relationship equity and trust calibration toward an autonomous agent. Results showed that early exposure to agent failures shapes more appropriately calibrated trust, offering practical guidance for onboarding operators to work with AI teammates.
recent_2: Dang, J., Shaw, T. H., & Helton, W. S. (2025). Does being away with the fairies really matter? The importance of thought content for task performance on a Go-No-Go target detection task. Applied Ergonomics, 129, 104607. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2025.104607
recent_2_synopsis: This experiment investigated whether the content of mind-wandering episodes (task-unrelated vs. task-related thoughts) differentially impairs performance on a Go/No-Go sustained attention task. The findings highlight that not all off-task cognition is equally detrimental, with implications for understanding vigilance decrements in operational settings.
recent_3: Mouloua, S. A., Helton, W. S., Matthews, G., & Shaw, T. H. (2024). Self-control enhances vigilance performance in temporally irregular tasks: an fNIRS frontoparietal investigation. Frontiers in Neuroergonomics, 5, 1415089. DOI: 10.3389/fnrgo.2024.1415089
recent_3_synopsis: Using functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS), this study demonstrated that individuals high in trait self-control maintain better vigilance performance in temporally irregular tasks and show more efficient frontoparietal neural resource allocation. The work provides neuroimaging evidence linking individual differences in self-regulation to sustained attention capacity.
seminal_1: Shaw, T. H., Warm, J. S., Finomore, V., Tripp, L., Matthews, G., Weiler, E., & Parasuraman, R. (2009). Effects of sensory modality on cerebral blood flow velocity during vigilance. Neuroscience Letters, 461(3), 207–211. DOI: 10.1016/j.neulet.2009.06.008
seminal_1_synopsis: This landmark study used Transcranial Doppler Sonography to show that cerebral blood flow velocity declines during vigilance tasks across both visual and auditory modalities, providing physiological evidence that sustained attention is neurologically taxing regardless of input channel. With approximately 92 citations, this paper established a key neuroergonomic marker of the vigilance decrement and has been foundational for subsequent work linking cerebral hemodynamics to operator performance.
seminal_2: Kohn, S. C., de Visser, E. J., Wiese, E., Lee, Y.-C., & Shaw, T. H. (2021). Measurement of trust in automation: A narrative review and reference guide. Frontiers in Psychology, 12, 604977. DOI: 10.3389/fpsyg.2021.604977
seminal_2_synopsis: This comprehensive narrative review catalogued and evaluated the self-report instruments, behavioral measures, and physiological indices used to assess human trust in automated systems, providing a consolidated methodological reference for the field. With approximately 254 citations (Crossref; ~445 on Google Scholar), it has become the standard citation for researchers selecting trust measurement tools in human–automation interaction studies.
expertise_overview: Research centers on the psychophysiology of vigilance and sustained attention, with particular focus on how cognitive resource depletion, individual differences in self-control, and task parameters (temporal regularity, sensory modality, signal probability) shape the vigilance decrement. Neuroimaging methods—Transcranial Doppler Sonography, Transcranial Cerebral Oximetry, and functional near-infrared spectroscopy—are used to index cerebral hemodynamic changes during prolonged monitoring tasks, linking observable performance decrements to underlying neural resource expenditure. A parallel research stream examines human–automation and human–autonomy teaming, investigating how trust is calibrated, repaired after violations, and managed when operators supervise multiple autonomous agents simultaneously. Work on mind-wandering and thought content explores how off-task cognition during Go/No-Go tasks affects commission and omission errors, connecting vigilance theory to everyday attentional failures. Application domains include aviation, unmanned systems supervision, human–robot interaction, and educational technology involving telepresence robots.
notes: Identity confirmed via George Mason University Department of Psychology faculty page (https://psychology.gmu.edu/people/tshaw4) and HFAC lab page (https://humanfactors.gmu.edu/people/tshaw4); also matches Google Scholar profile (user=aCY_eesAAAAJ). Shaw is Associate Professor in the Department of Psychology at GMU; Ph.D. 2008, University of Cincinnati. Citation counts: Crossref counts as of June 2026 (seminal_1: 92; seminal_2: 254); Google Scholar shows ~445 for seminal_2 and ~3,873 total citations. All DOIs verified via Crossref API (June 2026): 10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104770 (recent_1) — verified; 10.1016/j.apergo.2025.104607 (recent_2) — verified; 10.3389/fnrgo.2024.1415089 (recent_3) — verified; 10.1016/j.neulet.2009.06.008 (seminal_1) — verified; 10.3389/fpsyg.2021.604977 (seminal_2) — verified. All five seed hf_papers DOIs also verified. No unverifiable citations.
